name: Chakhchoukha
search_query: Chakhchoukha Algeria traditional recipe sources official
notes: The AfroKitchen Chakhchoukha entry matches the core pattern: Algerian torn rougag or thin flatbread served with a tomato-based meat stew, chickpeas, onions, ras el hanout, chile heat, and a communal platter. The main practical gap was that the method used oil, stew seasoning, and water that were not visible in the ingredient list.

name: Couscous Royal
search_query: Couscous Royal Algeria traditional recipe sources official
notes: The recipe is directionally sound as an Algerian celebratory couscous page, but 'royal' is a flexible restaurant/diaspora style rather than a single fixed official formula. The page needed visible ingredients for onion, oil, water, salt, pepper, ras el hanout, and harissa because the cooking steps already use them.

name: Makroud
search_query: Makroud Algeria traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Makroud entry matches the main pattern: diamond semolina pastry, date filling with cinnamon and orange blossom water, frying, then honey coating. The practical gap was that the method used salt and warm water, while the ingredient list did not show them.

name: Calulu de Peixe
search_query: Calulu de Peixe Angola traditional recipe sources official
notes: Multiple sources identify fish calulu as layered fresh and dried fish with vegetables such as okra, tomato, garlic, greens, and palm oil. The AfroKitchen method keeps the layering logic and uses accessible spinach when sweet potato leaves are unavailable.

name: Funge de Bombo
search_query: Funge de Bombo Angola traditional recipe sources official
notes: References agree that funge is cassava flour whisked into boiling water and stirred until thick. The AfroKitchen method starts with a slurry to reduce lumps for home cooks.

name: Muamba de Galinha
search_query: Muamba de Galinha Angola traditional recipe sources official
notes: Common references agree on chicken, red palm oil or palm sauce, okra, tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and a starchy side such as funge. The AfroKitchen version keeps peanuts out of the core recipe to avoid confusing it with muamba de ginguba.

name: Amiwo
search_query: Amiwo Benin traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Amiwo entry matches the core Beninese pattern: cornmeal or corn flour cooked into red tomato-based paste, served with chicken or fish and sauce. The page needed garlic, water, salt, pepper, and a palm-oil/vegetable-oil wording because these appear across sources and in the cooking method.

name: Grilled Wagasi
search_query: Grilled Wagasi Benin traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Wagasi page is directionally sound: wagasi is a West African cow-milk cheese strongly associated with northern Benin and Parakou, commonly fried, boiled, or cooked in sauce, and often eaten with pepper. The practical gap was the piment sauce using lime and salt without listing them.

name: Kuli-Kuli
search_query: Kuli-Kuli Benin traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Kuli-Kuli entry matches the common West African method: roasted peanuts are ground into paste, excess oil is extracted, the paste is seasoned, shaped, and fried until crisp. No static recipe changes were needed in this pass.

name: Morogo
search_query: Morogo Botswana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The AfroKitchen Morogo page is directionally sound: morogo is a Botswana and Southern African leafy-green dish, often using wild spinach, cowpea leaves, amaranth, or pumpkin leaves, cooked with onion, tomato, oil, salt, and sometimes peanuts or peanut butter. The current peanut powder version is defensible as a richer morogo wa dinawa-style variation.

name: Seswaa
search_query: Seswaa Botswana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Seswaa page matches the cross-source pattern: beef or goat boiled until tender, shredded or pounded, seasoned very simply with salt and sometimes onion, pepper, or bay in modern home adaptations, then served with pap/bogobe and morogo.

name: Vetkoek with Mince
search_query: Vetkoek with Mince Botswana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Vetkoek with Mince page matches the Southern African pattern: yeast dough fried into golden rounds, split and filled with savory mince. The main fix was practical: the mince step used salt, pepper, and curry powder, but those seasonings were not visible in the ingredient list.

name: Babenda
search_query: Babenda Burkina Faso traditional recipe sources official
notes: The prior Babenda page had the right broad idea of greens and soumbala, but the grain base was off. Stronger Burkina sources describe modern Babenda as sorrel and African spinach or amaranth leaves cooked with rice, pounded peanuts, soumbala, dried fish, pepper, salt, and shea butter or oil for serving.

name: Poulet Bicyclette
search_query: Poulet Bicyclette Burkina Faso traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Poulet Bicyclette page is directionally sound: local free-range chicken grilled over charcoal with a sharp marinade of garlic, mustard or vinaigrette, citrus or vinegar, oil, chile, herbs, salt, and pepper. The main practical gap was missing salt, pepper, and herbs in the ingredient list, plus copy that over-explained the bicycle name.

name: Riz Gras
search_query: Riz Gras Burkina Faso traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Riz Gras page is directionally sound as a Burkina-style one-pot tomato rice with meat, oil, onion, tomato paste, vegetables, and rice. The practical gaps were garlic, water or stock, salt, pepper, and chile, all common in source patterns or used by the method.

name: Burundian Isombe
search_query: Burundian Isombe Burundi traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Isombe page matches the core pattern of cassava or manioc leaves simmered with aromatics and palm oil, often served with ubugari. The main gap was that many Burundi/Rwanda/Great Lakes recipes include peanut butter or pounded peanuts, and the page did not make that component visible.

name: Mukeke
search_query: Mukeke Burundi traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Mukeke page correctly identifies the dish as a prized Lake Tanganyika fish. The page should name mukeke first and tilapia only as a substitute, and it should include parsley/herb notes used in sauce or marinade references. Grilled and pan-sauced versions both appear in sources.

name: Ubugari
search_query: Ubugari Burundi traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Ubugari page is sound as a cassava-flour staple served with beans, isombe, fish, or meat sauces. The main improvement is to fill the empty serving field and make the optional oil or butter used in some recipes visible.

name: Eru
search_query: Eru Cameroon traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Eru page is sound: finely shredded eru or okok leaves cooked with waterleaf or spinach, palm oil, crayfish, smoked fish, cow skin or beef, and served with garri, water fufu, or fufu corn. No static recipe changes were needed.

name: Koki Beans
search_query: Koki Beans Cameroon traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Koki Beans page is sound on technique and ingredients. The only change is copy: sources place koki across Littoral, South-West, and West Cameroon rather than only a single Bamileke grasslands frame.

name: Ndolé
search_query: Ndolé Cameroon traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Ndole page is sound as a Cameroonian bitter-leaf and peanut stew. The main practical gap was missing water or broth, salt, and white/black pepper, while the method already depends on seasoning and peanut blending liquid.

name: Poulet DG
search_query: Poulet DG Cameroon traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on chicken, fried plantains, and a colorful vegetable sauce. The AfroKitchen version keeps the plantains separate until the end so they stay sweet-edged rather than mushy.

name: Cachupa Rica
search_query: Cachupa Rica Cape Verde traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Cachupa Rica page is sound: hominy corn, beans, vegetables, meats or sausage, and slow cooking. The method used garlic, bay leaves, salt, and water that were not visible in ingredients.

name: Canja de Galinha
search_query: Canja de Galinha Cape Verde traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Canja de Galinha page is directionally sound as a Cape Verdean chicken and rice soup used for comfort, sickness, funerals, and family occasions. It needed water, salt, pepper, and optional herbs visible in ingredients.

name: Pastel com Diablo Dentro
search_query: Pastel com Diablo Dentro Cape Verde traditional recipe sources official
notes: The previous Pastel com Diabo Dentro page had the right tuna/chile idea but the dough was too generic. Stronger Cape Verdean sources use sweet potato plus cornmeal for the dough, with a spicy tuna filling seasoned with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, vinegar or lemon, and salt.

name: Gozo
search_query: Gozo Central African Republic traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Gozo page is sound: cassava flour and water cooked into a stiff paste used with sauces and stews. No static recipe changes were needed.

name: Kanda ti Nyma
search_query: Kanda ti Nyma Central African Republic traditional recipe sources official
notes: The previous Kanda ti Nyma page had a peanut sauce, but missed the okra that distinguishes the kanda ti nyma pattern in multiple sources. It also used ground peanuts inside the meatballs, while sources more often bind the meatballs with egg and season them with garlic and chile, reserving peanuts for the sauce.

name: Sauce Graine
search_query: Sauce Graine Central African Republic traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Sauce Graine page is directionally sound as a palm-fruit sauce served with starch, fish, meat, and aromatics, but source coverage was not strongly CAR-specific. The practical gaps were palm oil for the saut? step and salt for seasoning.

name: Boule
search_query: Boule Chad traditional recipe sources official
notes: Boule is sound as a Chadian millet or sorghum flour staple cooked into a firm paste for sauces. The main improvement is presentation copy and clearer step wording.

name: Daraba
search_query: Daraba Chad traditional recipe sources official
notes: Daraba needed the full vegetable body made visible. Stronger Chadian-style references use okra, greens, tomato, peanut butter, and often sweet potato or eggplant with water or stock.

name: La Bouillie
search_query: La Bouillie Chad traditional recipe sources official
notes: The millet porridge structure is sound. It needed salt and softer presentation language, with milk kept as a finishing choice rather than the whole cooking liquid.

name: Langouste a la Vanille
search_query: Langouste a la Vanille Comoros traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Comorian lobster and vanilla idea is right, but the sauce should be lighter and more elegant. Cream is common, while coconut cream is a reasonable island-style option.

name: Mkatra Foutra
search_query: Mkatra Foutra Comoros traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old page treated Mkatra Foutra like a baked loaf. Stronger Comorian references describe it as a yeast coconut flatbread cooked on a pan and finished with sesame.

name: Pilao ya Nazi
search_query: Pilao ya Nazi Comoros traditional recipe sources official
notes: Pilao ya Nazi is sound as a Comorian coconut rice pilaf. It mostly needed serving context and calmer copy.

name: Liboke ya Mbika
search_query: Liboke ya Mbika Democratic Republic of Congo traditional recipe sources official
notes: Liboke ya Mbika is directionally sound as pumpkin seed paste steamed in banana leaves. It needed salt and a small blending-liquid cue made visible.

name: Moambe Chicken
search_query: Moambe Chicken Democratic Republic of Congo traditional recipe sources official
notes: Moambe Chicken is sound as chicken in palm nut sauce, but the page omitted oil, salt, pepper, and an optional okra cue found in Congolese versions.

name: Saka-Saka
search_query: Saka-Saka Democratic Republic of Congo traditional recipe sources official
notes: Saka-Saka is right on cassava leaves, palm oil, fish, and long cooking. The page needed the common peanut paste option and more visible salt/water balance.

name: Djiboutian Laxoox
search_query: Djiboutian Laxoox Djibouti traditional recipe sources official
notes: Laxoox is sound as a fermented one-sided flatbread. The fermentation timer was missing and the page needed serving context.

name: Fah-Fah
search_query: Fah-Fah Djibouti traditional recipe sources official
notes: Fah-Fah is sound as a Djiboutian goat or meat soup with vegetables and warming spices. It needed serving context and cleaner step titles.

name: Skoudehkaris
search_query: Skoudehkaris Djibouti traditional recipe sources official
notes: Skoudehkaris was missing tomato paste, chile warmth, black pepper, and herbs that appear in stronger Djibouti references. The one-pot lamb and rice base is sound.

name: Ful Medames
search_query: Ful Medames Egypt traditional recipe sources official
notes: Ful Medames is sound, but the cook time was inconsistent with the dried-bean method and salt was missing from the visible ingredient list.

name: Koshari
search_query: Koshari Egypt traditional recipe sources official
notes: Koshari was missing several ingredients used in the method: oil, cumin, salt, chile, and enough vinegar for both tomato sauce and dakka. Vermicelli is also a common rice component.

name: Molokhia
search_query: Molokhia Egypt traditional recipe sources official
notes: Molokhia is sound, but the broth aromatics used in the method were not visible in the ingredient list. The public copy also needed cleaner punctuation.

name: Ta'ameya
search_query: Ta'ameya Egypt traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources consistently identify Egyptian ta'ameya as fava-based, herb-rich, and fried. The AfroKitchen version uses soaked uncooked split favas, not canned beans, for proper texture.

name: Jollof Rice Equatoguineano
search_query: Jollof Rice Equatoguineano Equatorial Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: The page works as an Equatoguinean-style tomato rice, but the old ingredient list missed garlic, peppers, cooking liquid, salt, and pepper while the method already used some of them.

name: Pepesoup
search_query: Pepesoup Equatorial Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: Pepesup is well supported as an Equatorial Guinean spicy fish soup. The old page was missing plantain, tomato, salt, and citrus even though those fit the strongest references and its own method.

name: Succotash de Guinée
search_query: Succotash de Guinée Equatorial Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: Succotash is repeatedly listed in online Equatorial Guinea food guides, but its history is disputed and not strongly documented as a precolonial local dish. The page should keep the bean-corn stew useful while avoiding overclaiming.

name: Eritrean Ful Medames
search_query: Eritrean Ful Medames Eritrea traditional recipe sources official
notes: The ful page matches Eritrean and Horn breakfast patterns: fava beans mashed with cumin, olive oil, lemon, chile, tomato, berbere or awaze, and bread or injera.

name: Eritrean Shiro
search_query: Eritrean Shiro Eritrea traditional recipe sources official
notes: The shiro page is directionally sound, but the old default used niter kibbeh while also tagging the dish as vegan and dairy-free. Fasting shiro should use oil by default, with spiced butter as an optional non-fasting finish.

name: Zigini with Injera
search_query: Zigini with Injera Eritrea traditional recipe sources official
notes: Zigini is sound as an Eritrean berbere beef stew, but the old page used spiced butter while claiming dairy-free. Stronger references also commonly include tomato, stock, and browning the beef.

name: Incwancwa
search_query: Incwancwa Eswatini traditional recipe sources official
notes: Incwancwa is supported as a Swazi sour fermented cornmeal porridge. The current recipe is usable; the main fix is clearer wording that it may be spoonable or thinned to drink.

name: Sishwala with Emasi
search_query: Sishwala with Emasi Eswatini traditional recipe sources official
notes: The sishwala with emasi page is sound: stiff maize porridge served with sour milk is a core Swazi staple combination.

name: Umbhidvo Wetintsanga
search_query: Umbhidvo Wetintsanga Eswatini traditional recipe sources official
notes: Umbhidvo wetintsanga is sound as pumpkin leaves cooked with groundnuts. The current page needed only a small liquid/texture cleanup and stronger serving guidance.

name: Atkilt Wat
search_query: Atkilt Wat Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on cabbage, potatoes, carrots, turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger. The AfroKitchen method staggers the vegetables so potatoes cook through without collapsing the cabbage.

name: Doro Wat
search_query: Doro Wat Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Doro wat is sound: chicken, onions, berbere, niter kibbeh, tomato paste, lemon, and eggs. The old page incorrectly marked the default niter-kibbeh version as dairy-free.

name: Genfo
search_query: Genfo Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on thick flour porridge shaped with a center well of niter kibbeh and berbere. The AfroKitchen method adds flour gradually to prevent lumps.

name: Gomen
search_query: Gomen Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on collard greens cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, and Ethiopian spicing. The AfroKitchen version keeps the greens bright by cooking them just until tender.

name: Injera
search_query: Injera Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: The injera page is sound but the old ingredient list made absit look like a second flour mixture. Stronger sources describe fermenting teff batter, cooking a portion into absit, then returning it before cooking one side only.

name: Kik Alicha
search_query: Kik Alicha Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on yellow split peas, turmeric, onion, garlic, ginger, and a mild stew profile. The AfroKitchen version keeps it vegan by default.

name: Kitfo
search_query: Kitfo Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: The kitfo page matches the Ethiopian pattern: finely minced beef with mitmita and niter kibbeh, served raw, lightly warmed, or cooked with ayib and gomen. The main improvement is safer, clearer serving guidance.

name: Misir Wat
search_query: Misir Wat Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on red lentils, berbere, onion, garlic, ginger, and a stew-like simmer. The AfroKitchen version keeps it vegan but notes niter kibbeh as an optional substitute.

name: Shiro Wat
search_query: Shiro Wat Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources agree on shiro powder or chickpea flour cooked into a smooth stew with onion, garlic, oil, and berbere. The AfroKitchen version adds the flour gradually to prevent clumping.

name: Tibs
search_query: Tibs Ethiopia traditional recipe sources official
notes: References agree on small pieces of meat cooked quickly with onion and Ethiopian seasonings. The AfroKitchen version keeps berbere optional in amount because some tibs styles use awaze on the side instead of a heavily red sauce.

name: Mustard Chicken
search_query: Mustard Chicken Gabon traditional recipe sources official
notes: The mustard chicken page is plausible for Gabonese/French-influenced cooking, but the old ingredient list missed salt and pepper while the method used them.

name: Nyembwe Chicken
search_query: Nyembwe Chicken Gabon traditional recipe sources official
notes: Nyembwe chicken is strongly supported as Gabon's national palm-nut chicken dish. The old page needed salt, pepper, oil, and a slightly fuller sauce build.

name: Smoked Fish with Plantains
search_query: Smoked Fish with Plantains Gabon traditional recipe sources official
notes: Smoked fish and plantain are strongly plausible Gabonese ingredients, but the exact named dish has thinner source coverage. The page needed salt, pepper, garlic, and liquid for the sauce.

name: Benachin
search_query: Benachin Gambia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Benachin is well supported as a Gambian one-pot rice dish related to thieboudienne. The old page had the right frame, but the ingredient list did not show oil, tomato, garlic, chile, salt, pepper, or cooking liquid clearly enough.

name: Domoda
search_query: Domoda Gambia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Domoda is strongly supported as Gambian groundnut stew with beef or chicken, peanut paste, tomatoes, tomato paste, onion, pumpkin or sweet potato, chile, and rice. The old page needed salt, pepper, lemon, and water made visible.

name: Supakanja
search_query: Supakanja Gambia traditional recipe sources official
notes: Supakanja is plausible and locally recognized as Gambian okra soup. The old page had the core okra, palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, and pepper pattern but needed salt, water, and optional leafy greens made explicit.

name: Banku and Tilapia
search_query: Banku and Tilapia Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The recipe follows the common Ghanaian plate: banku made from fermented corn and cassava dough, served with grilled or fried tilapia and pepper sauce.

name: Ghanaian Jollof Rice
search_query: Ghanaian Jollof Rice Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The Ghanaian jollof page is sound. The main gaps were missing salt, stock or water, bay leaf, and optional curry or thyme while the method depends on seasoning and liquid control.

name: Groundnut Soup
search_query: Groundnut Soup Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: Ghanaian groundnut soup is sound as a peanut, tomato, chile, ginger, and chicken soup for fufu or rice balls. The old page needed stock/water, garlic, tomato paste, and salt/pepper visible.

name: Kelewele
search_query: Kelewele Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: Kelewele is sound as Ghanaian spiced fried ripe plantain. The old page needed salt and the common garlic/onion spice paste option made visible.

name: Kontomire Stew
search_query: Kontomire Stew Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: References consistently identify kontomire as cocoyam leaves cooked into stew with palm oil, tomato, onion, pepper, egusi, and smoked or salted fish. The AfroKitchen version preserves those anchors and allows spinach as an access substitute.

name: Red Red
search_query: Red Red Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sources consistently frame red red as a Ghanaian black-eyed pea stew cooked with palm oil or vegetable oil and served with fried plantain.

name: Waakye
search_query: Waakye Ghana traditional recipe sources official
notes: The core profile is stable across sources: rice and beans cooked together with sorghum or millet leaves for color, then served with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, fish, meat, salad, or spaghetti.

name: Fouti
search_query: Fouti Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: The fouti page is directionally sound as a Guinean fonio staple. The main issue was the default butter conflicting with vegan and dairy-free tags.

name: Guinean Chicken Yassa
search_query: Guinean Chicken Yassa Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: Yassa is originally associated with Casamance/Senegal but is widely cooked across nearby West Africa, including Guinea. The old page had the lemon-onion-chicken structure but missed garlic, mustard, salt, pepper, and bay in ingredients.

name: Sauce Feuille
search_query: Sauce Feuille Guinea traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sauce feuille is sound as a West African leaf sauce, and Guinean sources support leafy sauces. The old page needed garlic, water or stock, and salt/pepper visible.

name: Caldo de Chabeu
search_query: Caldo de Chabeu Guinea-Bissau traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old page treated chabeu as jollof-style rice, but stronger Guinea-Bissau references describe caldo de chabeu as a palm-oil broth or stew with okra and meat or fish. The public recipe needed a full correction.

name: Caldo de Mancarra
search_query: Caldo de Mancarra Guinea-Bissau traditional recipe sources official
notes: Caldo de mancarra is strongly supported as Guinea-Bissau peanut broth or stew with chicken or fish, vegetables, and rice. The old page needed garlic, ginger, stock, oil, salt, and pepper visible.

name: Cashew Rice
search_query: Cashew Rice Guinea-Bissau traditional recipe sources official
notes: Cashews are central to Guinea-Bissau agriculture and food culture, but arroz de caju has thinner recipe-source coverage than the major national dishes. The page is useful as a cashew rice side, but it should avoid dairy-free conflict from default butter.

name: Alloco
search_query: Alloco Ivory Coast traditional recipe sources official
notes: Alloco was directionally correct, but the pantry details were too thin for a cookable page. The corrected recipe keeps ripe plantain, hot oil, piment, onion, tomato, and salt front and center.

name: Attiéké with Grilled Fish
search_query: Attiéké with Grilled Fish Ivory Coast traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old page had the right idea but missed several ingredients used in its own instructions. The corrected version gives the fish marinade, attiéké steaming, onion-tomato garnish, and piment enough detail to cook.

name: Kedjenou
search_query: Kedjenou Ivory Coast traditional recipe sources official
notes: The AfroKitchen Kedjenou entry matches the core cross-source pattern: Ivorian/Baoulé or Akan dish, chicken or guinea fowl, sealed low-heat cooking in a canari or tightly sealed pot, little or no added liquid, tomatoes/onions/chile/aromatics, and service with attiéké or rice.

name: Githeri
search_query: Githeri Kenya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The core dish is maize and beans boiled together. A light onion-tomato finish is a common modern approach, while the simple boiled version remains valid.

name: Kenyan Pilau
search_query: Kenyan Pilau Kenya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old pilau had the right ingredients but the sequence risked tough beef. The corrected method cooks the beef stock first, then builds the browned onion and spice base before steaming the rice.

name: Mukimo
search_query: Mukimo Kenya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The common pattern is potatoes mashed with maize, peas or beans, and greens. Onion or butter is optional, while pumpkin leaves or spinach provide the green color.

name: Nyama Choma
search_query: Nyama Choma Kenya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old nyama choma was tasty but read more like a heavy marinade than the Kenyan classic. The corrected version keeps salt, smoke, optional lemon, and kachumbari as the core.

name: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki
search_query: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki Kenya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The existing ugali and sukuma wiki page was solid. This pass mostly tightens ratios, sides, and language so the greens stay bright and the ugali stays firm.

name: Motoho
search_query: Motoho Lesotho traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old motoho sequence cooked the porridge before fermentation, which is not the strongest fit for sour sorghum porridge. The corrected version ferments a sorghum slurry first, then cooks it into a smooth breakfast porridge.

name: Nyekoe
search_query: Nyekoe Lesotho traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old nyekoe leaned toward samp and beans, but stronger Basotho references point to beans, sorghum, and pumpkin as the more distinctive nyekoe profile. The corrected version uses that highland combination.

name: Papa le Moroho
search_query: Papa le Moroho Lesotho traditional recipe sources official
notes: The existing papa le moroho recipe was close. The update keeps the everyday Basotho structure while improving water ratios, seasoning, and the greens method.

name: Liberian Jollof Rice
search_query: Liberian Jollof Rice Liberia traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old Liberian jollof was missing several common aromatics and vegetables. The corrected version keeps the Liberian mixed-protein feel while adding bell pepper, garlic, stock, thyme, bay, carrot, green beans, and cabbage.

name: Palm Butter Soup
search_query: Palm Butter Soup Liberia traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old palm butter soup captured the main idea but needed stronger seasoning and liquid guidance. The corrected version makes palm fruit concentrate, meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, onion, and okra or bitterball cook as a coherent Liberian soup.

name: Torborgee
search_query: Torborgee Liberia traditional recipe sources official
notes: The old torborgee page overfocused on locust beans and did not reflect the better documented Liberian beans or bitter vegetable torborgee pattern. The corrected version makes it a thick palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, hot pepper, and rice.

name: Asida
search_query: Asida Libya traditional recipe sources official
notes: The existing asida was broadly correct. This pass adds date syrup as the more Libyan serving option and keeps the flour-water-butter structure simple.

name: Bazin
search_query: Bazin Libya traditional recipe sources official
notes: Bazin was mostly sound, but the spice profile was too thin for Libyan sauce. The update keeps barley dough, lamb tomato stew, potato, eggs, and adds the expected warm spices.

name: Sharba Libiya
search_query: Sharba Libiya Libya traditional recipe sources official
notes: Sharba Libiya needed missing liquid, oil, chickpeas, lemon, and parsley/cilantro details. The update keeps lamb, tomato paste, orzo, dried mint, and Ramadan-soup identity.

name: Mofo Gasy
search_query: Mofo Gasy Madagascar traditional recipe sources official
notes: Mofo Gasy was ingredient-correct but the total time ignored fermentation. The update keeps rice flour, yeast, sugar, coconut milk, and mold cooking while making the rise time honest.

name: Ravitoto
search_query: Ravitoto Madagascar traditional recipe sources official
notes: Ravitoto needed a longer braise time. Cassava leaves and pork should cook slowly until tender, rich, and earthy.

name: Romazava
search_query: Romazava Madagascar traditional recipe sources official
notes: Romazava was close but needed anamalaho or paracress named clearly. The update keeps beef, tomato, onion, ginger, mixed greens, and rice, while avoiding extra vegetables that would drift away from the Malagasy profile.

name: Kondowole
search_query: Kondowole Malawi traditional recipe sources official
notes: Kondowole was mostly correct. This pass keeps cassava flour and water, adds stronger fish-relish pairing language, and removes halal as unnecessary metadata.

name: Mandasi
search_query: Mandasi Malawi traditional recipe sources official
notes: Mandasi needed honest rise time. The ingredients are plausible for a Malawian/East African fried dough, but the old 40-minute total ignored the one-hour proof.

name: Nsima with Chambo
search_query: Nsima with Chambo Malawi traditional recipe sources official
notes: Nsima with Chambo was close, but it should name chambo as the preferred fish with tilapia as a substitute and should not advertise halal automatically.

name: Fakoye
search_query: Fakoye Mali traditional recipe sources official
notes: Fakoye needed its leaf-sauce identity corrected. Okra can support texture, but the defining ingredient is fakoye or jute leaf, often with meat or dried fish and soumbala.

name: Tigadegena
search_query: Tigadegena Mali traditional recipe sources official
notes: Tigadegena needed garlic, liquid, and vegetable range. The corrected version keeps peanut paste, tomato, meat, and optional vegetables while making the sauce easier to cook without scorching.

name: Toh
search_query: Toh Mali traditional recipe sources official
notes: Toh was already in good shape. This pass keeps millet or sorghum flour, water, salt, and steady stirring as the full recipe.

name: Lakh
search_query: Lakh Mauritania traditional recipe sources official
